text: The surgeon uses the left hand to apply pressure from above on the contents of the hernia sac, while laparoscopic instruments are used to carefully separate adhesions and incarcerated omentum.

The surgeon uses two laparoscopic ports, including a harmonic scalpel and a telescope, to perform the hernia repair. Gravity assists in the separation of tissue, allowing the omentum and fat content to be pulled back inside the abdomen without injuring the skin.

The surgeon continues to press the hernia content completely inside using the left hand, aiding in the reduction of the large hernia. The procedure involves an eye palm hernia surgery technique, where the hernia sac is left intact, and only the hernia content is removed.

The surgeon uses a harmonic instrument to clear omental adhesions in the lower abdomen, ensuring the mesh can project six centimeters beyond the healthy margin of the hernia defect. The round ligament is visible on the right side, fixed to the anterior wall, which may be separated or left intact.

The surgeon clears adhesions around the peripheral margin of six centimeters from the hernia defect. A polyurethane mesh with polyester on one side is prepared for application. Gore-Tex sutures are mapped on the mesh's corners, centered over the defect. A small stab wound is made with an Alebu knife, and sutures are pulled through using suture passers for trans-facial fixation. Each corner is fixed separately, ensuring bilateral symmetry, with at least one centimeter of rectus muscle and peritoneum between the mesh and tissue.

The surgeon adjusts the intra-abdominal pressure to eight millimeters of mercury to facilitate the release of gas post-surgery, ensuring the abdominal wall remains loose without compromising vision. The final corner of the mesh is secured subcutaneously at Palmer's point, completing the fixation process.

The surgeon completes the fixation of the mesh by pulling and tying the sutures at all four corners, ensuring bilateral symmetry and proper stretch of the mesh. The pressure is decreased to facilitate this process. A titanium clip is applied using a protack device, with a grasper providing support during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a protract device to fix the mesh, starting with the most remote point from the operator. The tacker is positioned at a two-centimeter distance, with the left hand supporting the abdominal wall to ensure the tacker tip faces directly opposite the wall for proper fixation. The mesh is spread from the center to the periphery to avoid corrugation, while avoiding the inferior epigastric vessel by firing either lateral or medial to it.

The surgeon performs inner crowning by decreasing the pressure of the abdominal wall to ensure proper fixation of the mesh. The tacker is carefully fired around the mesh, avoiding the defect area to prevent infection. The gas is then removed from the abdominal cavity.
